This post is going to be a relaxed intro to web scraping, so it'll be shorter than the previous ones — mainly to give the brain a break.
In short, a web scraper (Web Crawler) is an automated program that searches and collects data on the web, then stores it locally or in a database.
Web scrapers are also called web spiders, ants, automatic indexers, etc. So if you hear someone say "web spider", they mean web scraper.
Why do we need scrapers? Because sometimes pages don't provide an API but we still want the data — that's when we use a scraper.
Probably hard to follow? Let me give an example. Say we want the article list from
, but it doesn't provide an API to get the article list — we can use a scraper to get it.
Real-life examples? Search engines like Google, Yahoo!, Bing all use scrapers to get web page data and build indexes, so users can search by keyword.

Of course search engines aren't the only example. Price comparison sites also use scrapers to get product data and build indexes, so users can search products by keyword. This involves things like algorithms — how to find the most popular product matching the user's keyword.
Quick summary of scraper use cases:
But heads up — scrapers are convenient and let us scrape data we want, but you also have to watch what you're scraping. If your scraping is unauthorized — like:
…then you might face legal liability. There's a saying:
"Scrape too well and you'll be eating jail food."

Some sites also have scraper defenses, since scrapers consume site resources. So when implementing, you'll find some sites can't be scraped — and you'll have to figure out how to bypass the defenses, which is one of scraping's challenges.
Now let's talk about the scraping flow. Roughly three steps:
How do we get data? Scraping behavior is similar to your everyday browsing:
You'll notice scraping behavior is really similar to your everyday browsing — we're just using code to perform these actions instead of humans.

How do we parse the data? This is where HTML knowledge comes in — we use HTML tags to parse data. For example, to get an article list, we have to find the article list's tag, then use it to get the article data.
So if you're a frontend dev, HTML tags shouldn't be unfamiliar — when we write web pages, we use HTML tags to build the page structure, and scrapers also use HTML tags to parse data.

The final "store the data" step is simpler — convert the scraped and parsed data to whatever format we want — JSON, CSV, XML, etc. Then use the data for analysis or store in a database for other uses.
You can also use scrapers to write stock analysis tools — pair scrapers with stock data, then analyze stock trends. That's also a scraping use case.
So scraping is actually pretty fun — you just need to know which sites can be scraped and which can't.
"How am I supposed to know that?"
Some sites explicitly say no scraping
Sites usually put a robots.txt at the root explaining which pages can be scraped. If you don't know what robots.txt is, see
Scrapers consume site resources — avoid putting excessive load on the site's server.
Don't need to elaborate. If you scrape sites with private data, you're committing a crime.
If an API is provided, use the API first
Some sites provide APIs. Use APIs first — reduces load on the site's server.
These are the main things to watch out for when implementing scrapers.
So the scraping concept is: use code to mimic human behavior to get the data we want. That's why the scraping flow is similar to your everyday browsing.
That's it for this post, see you in the next one.
